root_text.sn47.45:1.1: “‘Kusalarāsī’ti, bhikkhave, vadamāno cattāro satipaṭṭhāne sammā vadamāno vadeyya.
root_text.sn47.45:1.2: Kevalo hāyaṁ, bhikkhave, kusalarāsi, yadidaṁ—cattāro satipaṭṭhānā.
root_text.sn47.45:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.45:2.5: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.45:2.6: ‘Kusalarāsī’ti, bhikkhave, vadamāno ime cattāro satipaṭṭhāne sammā vadamāno vadeyya.
root_text.sn47.45:2.7: Kevalo hāyaṁ, bhikkhave, kusalarāsi, yadidaṁ—cattāro satipaṭṭhānā”ti.

translation_text.sn47.45:1.1: “Rightly speaking, mendicants, you’d call these four kinds of mindfulness meditation a ‘heap of the skillful’.
translation_text.sn47.45:1.2: For these four kinds of mindfulness meditation are entirely a heap of the skillful.
translation_text.sn47.45:2.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.45:2.3: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.45:2.5: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.45:2.6: Rightly speaking, you’d call these four kinds of mindfulness meditation a ‘heap of the skillful’.
translation_text.sn47.45:2.7: For these four kinds of mindfulness meditation are entirely a heap of the skillful.”
